title: Modular non-Hermitian topology and its application to critical sensing
authors: Saubhik Sarkar, Chiranjib Mukhopadhyay, Abolfazl Bayat
abstract: Non-Hermitian topological systems have attracted a lot of research activities in recent times, both theoretically and experimentally, due to their unique physical properties and association with open quantum systems. We show that modular structures, where specific couplings at regular intervals take distinct values, enrich the unique topological attributes of these systems such as the non-Hermitian skin effect and the breakdown of conventional bulk-boundary correspondence. These systems also possess the capability of displaying criticality-enhanced sensitivity for precision metrology. We establish how the modular structure enhances their sensing performance near spectral topological phase transitions and show that the enhancement can be achieved in multi-parameter estimation scenarios as well.
summary: This paper explores how incorporating modular couplings into non-Hermitian lattice models enriches their topological features, particularly concerning the non-Hermitian skin effect. By analyzing spectral topological phase transitions associated with point gap closure, the authors demonstrate that these systems exhibit enhanced, Heisenberg-limited sensitivity for quantum sensing, even when estimating multiple unknown parameters simultaneously.
main_problem: The paper investigates how introducing modular structures enriches the topological properties of non-Hermitian systems, specifically focusing on the non-Hermitian skin effect and its application to enhancing quantum sensing.
main_result: The modular structure allows for the identification of critical points where spectral topological phase transitions occur, leading to a measurable enhancement in sensing performance, even in multi-parameter estimation scenarios.
method: The analysis employs the Generalized Brillouin Zone (GBZ) formalism and calculates topological invariants like the winding number to characterize phase transitions, alongside applying quantum estimation theory (QFIM) for sensing.
model_or_system: The model is a general 1D tight-binding non-Hermitian Hamiltonian, extended to include modular couplings ($J_m, J'_m$) across $L$ modules. Specific examples include the modular Hatano-Nelson and SSH models.
key_observables: Non-Hermitian skin effect, point/line gaps, winding number, Quantum Fisher Information Matrix (QFIM), and the scaling exponent $b$ of sensing metrics.
important_parameters: Modular couplings ($J_m, J'_m$), module size ($r$), and the ratio of non-reciprocal couplings ($J_R/J_L$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the GBZ formalism to correctly map the bulk spectrum to the open boundary condition (OBC) spectrum, and the sensing analysis assumes the probe state is the right eigenstate with the largest imaginary eigenvalue.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the spectral topological phase transitions (point gap closure) for modular models, showing the skin effect vanishing at critical points, and demonstrate the quadratic scaling of QFIM elements for enhanced sensing.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the general NH model and its topological features (skin effect, gap closure) using the GBZ formalism, to analyzing the specific role of modular couplings in controlling these transitions, and finally applying this framework to derive and quantify the enhanced sensitivity in multi-parameter quantum estimation.
why_interesting: This work directly connects topological phase transitions (a core concept in condensed matter theory) to practical quantum sensing limits, providing a concrete physical mechanism (modular structure) to engineer superior metrology performance in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the model. Each module consists of r sites and each site has d sublevels. The modules, sites, and sublevels are denoted by dashed rectangles, grey ellipses, and colored circles, respectively. The NH tunneling strengths between the modules are Jm and J′ m while those between sites are JL and JR. Coupling between adjacent sublevels is J0 and is Hermitian. The numbers of sublevels d=1 and d=2 correspond to modular Hatano-Nelson and NH SSH models, respectively.

FIG. 2. Spectral topological phase transition and point gap structure. Left panel shows the case of modular Hatano-Nelson model with d=1, r=3, L=50. Here Jm=J=1/J′ m and J=2JL. (a) The point gap structure at JR= −2.5JL is shown by three loops corresponding to the three bands of the model with PBC. (b) The presence of point gap with PBC corresponds to NH skin effect for OBC, which is shown by the exponential edge localization of the cumulative population P j of the all the eigenstates at site j. (c) The closure of point gap at JR= −2JL. (d) The corresponding vanishing of skin effect shown by the delocalization of the cumulative population. Right panel shows the case of modular NH SSH model...

FIG. 3. Band topological phase transition and line gap structure. The band formation in the spectrum corresponding to different boundary conditions are shown for d=2, r=2, L=50, J0=1.25JL. Here Jm=JL+J, J′ m=JR+J and J=2JL. For band structure with PBC, (a), (d), (g) show real, imaginary, and absolute energies, respectively. The vertical lines denote JR values for different types of line gap closures. The magenta circle is for JR=0.6008JL. Here closure of the line gap between the two central bands are shown in (j). The cyan cross is for JR= −1.3822JL. Here closure of the line gap between the central band and each of the side bands are shown in (k). The grey triangle is for JR= −2.6008JL....

FIG. 4. Single parameter sensing. Top panel correspond to modular Hatano-Nelson model and bottom panel correspond to modu- lar SSH model. (a, e) QFI and CFI (in the position basis) at the point gap closure. Blue curves correspond to the gap closing for the modular case. Red curves correspond to the non-modular case where the point gap closes at JR= −JL. For modular Hatano-Nelson model, gap closes at JR= −0.4JL for d=1, r=3, L=50, Jm=J=1/J′ m and J=0.4JL. For modular SSH model, gap closes at JR= −0.5JL for d=2, r=2, L=50, J0=2JL, Jm=JL+J, J′ m=JR+J and J=0.5JL. (b, f) The peak QFI FQ max value for the modular case as a function of J. Red dashed line shows the non-modular value. (c, g) The...

FIG. 5. Multi-parameter sensing. Here we consider d=1, r=3, L=100, Jm=J=1/J′ m. Considering JR=Jx+iJy, we look at the QFIM F Q(Jx, Jy) in the top panel. Blue and red curves show modular and non-modular cases, respectively. (a) F Q Jx,Jx with F C Jx,Jx in the current operator basis. (b) Tr(F Q −1) with Tr(F C −1) in the current operator basis. (c) F Q Jx,Jx, F Q Jy,Jx, and Tr(F Q −1) as functions of J. (d) Quadratic scaling for F Q Jx,Jx,
